entry.title: CodeSage for SAP - (Building an AI-Powered ABAP Knowledge Platform from Scratch)
entry.content:
What is CodeSage for SAP:

The most prominent technical use of "CodeSage" is for a series of open-source embedding models developed for software engineering tasks like code search and representation learning. Using the same concept wants to create a code knowledge assistant for ABAP development teams. It gives every developer — new or experienced — the ability to query your organization's entire codebase in plain English and receive accurate, contextualized answers grounded in your own source code.

Who will build the CodeSage for SAP:

This artifact will be very useful for multiple purposes, such as learning and project delivery etc. It could be built as a project by institutions, large orgs running on SAP.  This is trained on specific ABAP landscape: naming conventions, Codes, error classes, enhancement patterns, clean core approach, SAP best practice etc.

How to build CodeSage for SAP:

Phase

What It Builds

Runs Where

Phase 1

Extracts ABAP source from SAP via RFC

Windows PC

Phase 2

Embeds code into ChromaDB vector index

Windows PC (CPU)

Phase 3

Fine-tunes LLaMA-3 with QLoRA, deploys to BYOM

Kaggle (free GPU)

Phase 4

Deploys CAP agent on BTP for developer queries

SAP BTP

 PHASE 1  —  Scan & Extract

Phase 1 reads your SAP system and saves every custom ABAP object (Z*/Y* namespace) as plain text files on your local Windows machine. The output is a folder of .abap source files and .json metadata sidecars that feed both Phase 2 and Phase 3.

Think of it as taking a complete photograph of your codebase at a point in time. The extractor uses pyrfc — a Python library that speaks SAP's RFC protocol — to call standard SAP function modules (RPY_PROGRAM_READ, RPY_FUNCTIONMODULE_READ) and pull source code line by line. A credential filter strips any hard-coded passwords before files are written to disk.

Artifacts:

Folder Structure

scan_config.yaml — controls what gets extracted

sap:
  host:     "your-sap-host"
  sysnr:    "00"
  client:   "100"
  user:     "CODESAGE_RFC"
  password: "${SAP_RFC_PASSWORD}"    # Never store passwords in plain text

output_dir: "./abap_files"

namespaces:
  - "Z*"
  - "Y*"

object_types:
  - PROG    # Programs / Reports
  - FUGR    # Function Groups (contains Function Modules)
  - CLAS    # ABAP Classes
  - INTF    # Interfaces
  - ENHO    # BAdI Implementations

exclude_packages:
  - "$TMP"  # Skip local dev objects

extractor.py — RFC extraction script

import pyrfc, json, pathlib, yaml, re

cfg = yaml.safe_load(open('scan_config.yaml'))
out = pathlib.Path(cfg['output_dir'])
out.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

# Strip any hard-coded credentials before saving to disk
CRED_RE = re.compile(
r'(password|passwd|pwd|secret|apikey)\s*=\s*[\'\"][^\'\"]+[\'\"]', re.I)

conn = pyrfc.Connection(
ashost=cfg['sap']['host'], sysnr=cfg['sap']['sysnr'],
client=cfg['sap']['client'], user=cfg['sap']['user'],
passwd=cfg['sap']['password'])

def save_object(name, obj_type, source, meta):
source_clean = CRED_RE.sub('[REDACTED]', source)
(out / f'{name}.abap').write_text(source_clean, encoding='utf-8')
(out / f'{name}.json').write_text(json.dumps(meta, indent=2), encoding='utf-8')

# Extract Programs
progs = conn.call('RPY_DIRECTORY_FINISH', OBJECT_TYPE='PROG', GENERIC_NAME='Z*')
for obj in progs['TADIR']:
try:
r = conn.call('RPY_PROGRAM_READ', PROG_NAME=obj['OBJ_NAME'])
src='\n'.join(l['LINE'] for l in r['SOURCE'])
save_object(obj['OBJ_NAME'], 'PROG', src, {
'name': obj['OBJ_NAME'], 'type': 'PROG',
'package': obj.get('DEVCLASS',''), 'changed': obj.get('LDATE','')})
except Exception as e:
print(f' SKIP {obj["OBJ_NAME"]}: {e}')

# Extract Function Modules
fugs = conn.call('RPY_DIRECTORY_FINISH', OBJECT_TYPE='FUGR', GENERIC_NAME='Z*')
for obj in fugs['TADIR']:
try:
r = conn.call('RPY_FUNCTIONMODULE_READ', FUNCNAME=obj['OBJ_NAME'])
src='\n'.join(l['LINE'] for l in r.get('SOURCE',[]))
save_object(obj['OBJ_NAME'], 'FUGR', src, {
'name': obj['OBJ_NAME'], 'type': 'FM',
'package': obj.get('DEVCLASS',''), 'changed': obj.get('LDATE','')})
except Exception as e:
print(f' SKIP {obj["OBJ_NAME"]}: {e}')

print(f'Done. {len(list(out.glob("*.abap")))} objects saved to {out}/')

Prerequisites & How to Run

Step 1 — Install SAP NW RFC SDK

# 1. Download "SAP NW RFC SDK 7.50 Windows 64-bit" from SAP Support Portal (Note 2573790)
# 2. Extract to C:\nwrfcsdk\  (you should see C:\nwrfcsdk\lib\sapnwrfc.dll)
# 3. Add to Windows PATH:
#    Win+S -> "Edit the system environment variables" -> Environment Variables
#    -> System Variables -> Path -> Edit -> New -> C:\nwrfcsdk\lib -> OK
# 4. Verify in a NEW PowerShell window:
Get-Item "C:\nwrfcsdk\lib\sapnwrfc.dll"   # Should list the file

Step 2 — Create virtual environment and install dependencies

cd C:\codesage\phase1_extract

python -m venv codesage-env
.\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1
# If blocked by execution policy, run this first:
Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser

pip install pyrfc pyyaml
python -c "import pyrfc; print('pyrfc OK')"
python -c "import yaml;  print('pyyaml OK')"

Step 3 — Store SAP password as environment variable

# Current session only (recommended for dev/test):
$env:SAP_RFC_PASSWORD = "your_sap_password_here"

# Persist across reboots (stored in Windows registry):
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable(
    "SAP_RFC_PASSWORD", "your_sap_password_here", "User")

Step 4 — Test RFC connection

# Save as test_connection.py, then run: python test_connection.py
import pyrfc, os, yaml
cfg  = yaml.safe_load(open('scan_config.yaml'))
conn = pyrfc.Connection(
    ashost=cfg['sap']['host'],   sysnr=cfg['sap']['sysnr'],
    client=cfg['sap']['client'], user=cfg['sap']['user'],
    passwd=os.environ['SAP_RFC_PASSWORD'])
result = conn.call('RFC_PING')
print('RFC connection successful:', result)
# Expected:  RFC connection successful: {}

python test_connection.py

Step 5 — Run the extractor

cd C:\codesage\phase1_extract
python extractor.py
# Console shows: SKIP messages for deleted/inaccessible objects (normal)
# Finishes with: Done. 8,432 objects saved to .\abap_files\

Step 6 — Verify output

# Count extracted files
(Get-ChildItem -Path ".\abap_files" -Filter "*.abap").Count
# Typical: 5,000 to 20,000

# Confirm no plain-text passwords in output
Select-String -Path ".\abap_files\*.abap" -Pattern "password" -CaseSensitive:$false |
  Where-Object { $_.Line -notmatch "REDACTED" }
# If nothing returned: credential filter is working correctly

Step 7 — Schedule monthly re-run (optional)

$action  = New-ScheduledTaskAction `
    -Execute "C:\codesage\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\python.exe" `
    -Argument "C:\codesage\phase1_extract\extractor.py" `
    -WorkingDirectory "C:\codesage\phase1_extract"
$trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -Monthly -DaysOfMonth 1 -At "02:00AM"
Register-ScheduledTask `
    -TaskName "CodeSage Phase1 Monthly Extract" `
    -Action $action -Trigger $trigger -RunLevel Highest

Phase1 Outcome:  A local folder (abap_files/) containing .abap source and .json metadata for every custom object in your SAP system (Z*/Y* namespace). Credentials are filtered, the RFC connection is read-only, and no data leaves your network. This folder is the shared input for Phase 2 (vector indexing) and Phase 3 (fine-tuning).

PHASE 2  —  Index & Embed

Phase 2 transforms the raw ABAP source files from Phase 1 into a searchable semantic vector database. 

Two steps run in sequence:

  • Step 1 — chunker.py:  Chunking: splits each ABAP file at logical boundaries (FORM/ENDFORM, METHOD/ENDMETHOD). One chunk = one function module, one method, or one report section. 150–500 tokens per chunk.
  • Step 2 — embedder.py:  Embedding: converts each chunk to a 768-number vector using the nomic-embed-text model running locally via Ollama. Stores vectors + original text in ChromaDB (4 collections).

The four ChromaDB collections:

Collection

Content

Chunk Unit

abap_programs

Executable reports and includes

One FORM routine or report body

function_modules

Function module source + parameters

One complete function module

abap_classes

ABAP OO classes, interfaces, methods

One METHOD block

documentation

SAP Help, Clean Core guidelines (optional)

One documentation paragraph

 Artifacts:

Folder Structure

chunker.py — split ABAP source into logical segments

import pathlib, json, re
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Chunk:
    text: str; object_name: str; chunk_type: str; collection: str

FORM_RE      = re.compile(r'^FORM\s+(\w+)', re.M | re.I)
METHOD_RE    = re.compile(r'^\s*METHOD\s+(\w+)', re.M | re.I)
ENDFORM_RE   = re.compile(r'^ENDFORM', re.M | re.I)
ENDMETHOD_RE = re.compile(r'^\s*ENDMETHOD', re.M | re.I)

def chunk_abap(abap_path, meta):
    src=abap_path.read_text(encoding='utf-8')
    lines = src.splitlines()
    name  = meta['name'];  typ = meta['type']
    chunks = []

    if typ == 'FM':                             # Function module = one chunk
        chunks.append(Chunk(src, name, 'FUNCTION', 'function_modules'))

    elif typ == 'CLAS':                         # Class = split on METHOD/ENDMETHOD
        buf, mname = [], None
        for line in lines:
            m = METHOD_RE.match(line)
            if m:
                mname = m.group(1); buf = [line]
            elif ENDMETHOD_RE.match(line) and mname:
                buf.append(line)
                chunks.append(Chunk('\n'.join(buf), f'{name}.{mname}',
                                    'METHOD', 'abap_classes'))
                mname = None; buf = []
            elif mname:
                buf.append(line)
        if not chunks:
            chunks.append(Chunk(src, name, 'CLASS_DEF', 'abap_classes'))

    else:                                       # Program = split on FORM/ENDFORM
        buf, fname = [], None
        for line in lines:
            m = FORM_RE.match(line)
            if m:
                fname = m.group(1); buf = [line]
            elif ENDFORM_RE.match(line) and fname:
                buf.append(line)
                chunks.append(Chunk('\n'.join(buf), f'{name}.{fname}',
                                    'FORM', 'abap_programs'))
                fname = None; buf = []
            elif fname:
                buf.append(line)
        chunks.append(Chunk(src[:3000], name, 'BODY', 'abap_programs'))

    return [c for c in chunks if len(c.text.strip()) > 50]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    abap_dir = pathlib.Path('../phase1_extract/abap_files')
    all_chunks = []
    for abap_file in abap_dir.glob('*.abap'):
        meta_file = abap_file.with_suffix('.json')
        meta = json.loads(meta_file.read_text()) if meta_file.exists()                else {'name': abap_file.stem, 'type': 'PROG'}
        all_chunks.extend(chunk_abap(abap_file, meta))
    print(f'Total chunks: {len(all_chunks)}')

embedder.py — embed chunks and store in ChromaDB

import chromadb, requests, json, pathlib
from chunker import chunk_abap

db = chromadb.PersistentClient(path='./chromadb_store')

COLLECTIONS = {
    'abap_programs':    db.get_or_create_collection('abap_programs'),
    'function_modules': db.get_or_create_collection('function_modules'),
    'abap_classes':     db.get_or_create_collection('abap_classes'),
    'documentation':    db.get_or_create_collection('documentation'),
}

def embed(text):
    """Call local nomic-embed-text via Ollama. No cloud, no API key."""
    r = requests.post('http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings',
                      json={'model': 'nomic-embed-text', 'prompt': text})
    return r.json()['embedding']           # 768-dimensional vector

def store_chunk(chunk, idx):
    col = COLLECTIONS[chunk.collection]
    vec = embed(chunk.text[:2000])
    col.add(
        ids=[f'{chunk.object_name}_{idx}'],
        embeddings=[vec],
        documents=[chunk.text],
        metadatas=[{'object': chunk.object_name, 'type': chunk.chunk_type}])

abap_dir = pathlib.Path('../phase1_extract/abap_files')
for i, abap_file in enumerate(sorted(abap_dir.glob('*.abap'))):
    meta_file = abap_file.with_suffix('.json')
    meta = json.loads(meta_file.read_text()) if meta_file.exists()            else {'name': abap_file.stem, 'type': 'PROG'}
    for j, chunk in enumerate(chunk_abap(abap_file, meta)):
        store_chunk(chunk, j)
    if i % 100 == 0:
        print(f'  Indexed {i} files...')

print('ChromaDB indexing complete.')
for name, col in COLLECTIONS.items():
    print(f'  {name}: {col.count()} chunks')

Prerequisites & How to Run 

Step 1 — Activate venv and install dependencies

cd C:\codesage\phase2_index
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

pip install chromadb requests
python -c "import chromadb; print('chromadb', chromadb.__version__)"

Step 2 — Install Ollama and download embedding model

# Install Ollama from ollama.com/download (Windows installer, free)
# After install, open a NEW PowerShell window:
ollama pull nomic-embed-text

ollama list
# NAME                    SIZE
# nomic-embed-text:latest 274 MB

Step 3 — Test Ollama is responding

# Save as test_ollama.py, run: python test_ollama.py
import requests
r = requests.post("http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings",
                  json={"model": "nomic-embed-text", "prompt": "hello SAP"})
vec = r.json()["embedding"]
print(f"Embedding OK: {len(vec)} dimensions")
# Expected: Embedding OK: 768 dimensions

python test_ollama.py

Step 4 — Dry-run chunker to preview chunk count

python chunker.py
# Expected: Total chunks: 42,318  (varies by codebase size)

Step 5 — Run the embedder (main Phase 2 step, ~2 hours on CPU)

python embedder.py
#   Indexed 0 files...  Indexed 100 files...  ...
# ChromaDB indexing complete.
#   abap_programs:    18,420 chunks
#   function_modules: 12,305 chunks
#   abap_classes:      9,841 chunks

Step 6 — Test semantic search

# Save as test_search.py, run: python test_search.py
import chromadb, requests

def embed(text):
    r = requests.post("http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings",
                      json={"model": "nomic-embed-text", "prompt": text})
    return r.json()["embedding"]

db  = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chromadb_store")
col = db.get_collection("function_modules")
results = col.query(
    query_embeddings=[embed("validate vendor payment terms")],
    n_results=3)
for doc_text, meta in zip(results["documents"][0], results["metadatas"][0]):
    print(f"[{meta['object']}]  {doc_text[:80]}...")
# Expected top result: [Z_VALIDATE_VENDOR_PAYTERMS]  FUNCTION Z_VALIDATE...

python test_search.py

Phase2 Outcome:  ChromaDB running on-premise with 4 collections and tens of thousands of embedded ABAP chunks. Any developer query can be matched to the most relevant code in your system in under 100 ms — without sending a single line of code to the cloud. This vector index is used at runtime (Phase 4) to feed context into every LLM response. 

PHASE 3  —  Fine-Tune, Merge & Package for BYOM

Phase 3 is the heart of CodeSage. By the end of it, you have a version of LLaMA-3 that has learned your naming conventions, your preferred BAPIs, your error class patterns, and your ABAP coding style.

Artifacts:

Folder Structure

generate_qa.py — Step A: create training dataset via Claude

import anthropic, json, pathlib

client = anthropic.Anthropic()     # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var

def generate_qa(chunk_text, object_name):
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            f"You are an ABAP expert. Given this code from {object_name}, "
            f"write 4 developer questions whose answer is in this code.\n\n"
            f"CODE:\n{chunk_text}\n\nReturn a JSON list of strings."}])
    questions = json.loads(msg.content[0].text)
    return [{"prompt": q, "completion": chunk_text} for q in questions]

with open("training_data.jsonl", "w") as f:
    for path in pathlib.Path("../phase1_extract/abap_files").glob("*.abap"):
        chunk = path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")[:2000]
        for pair in generate_qa(chunk, path.stem):
            f.write(json.dumps(pair) + "\n")
# Result: 30,000 - 50,000 lines in training_data.jsonl

train_qlora.py — Step B: QLoRA fine-tuning 

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, TrainingArguments, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType
from trl import SFTTrainer
import torch

MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"

# Load base model in 4-bit NF4 (reduces GPU memory from ~28 GB to ~6-12 GB)
bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True)

model     = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID,
              quantization_config=bnb_config, device_map="auto")
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID)

# Attach LoRA adapters — only these tiny matrices will be trained
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16, lora_alpha=32,
    target_modules=["q_proj","k_proj","v_proj","o_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.05, bias="none",
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# Output: trainable params: 13,631,488 (0.17% of 8B)

# Train
trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer,
    train_dataset=dataset,
    dataset_text_field="text",
    max_seq_length=2048,
    args=TrainingArguments(
        output_dir="./lora-adapter",
        num_train_epochs=3,
        per_device_train_batch_size=4,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
        learning_rate=2e-4,
        fp16=True, save_strategy="epoch", logging_steps=50))
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./lora-adapter")   # ~50 MB adapter file

merge_model.py — Step C: merge LoRA adapter into base model

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from peft import PeftModel

base      = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
    torch_dtype="auto", device_map="cpu")
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct")

model  = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "./lora-adapter/")
merged = model.merge_and_unload()        # adapter deltas baked into base weights

merged.save_pretrained("./merged_model/")
tokenizer.save_pretrained("./merged_model/")
print("Merge complete. ./merged_model/ (~16 GB) ready for Docker packaging.")

Dockerfile — Step 'D: vLLM container for SAP AI Core BYOM

FROM vllm/vllm-openai:latest
COPY ./merged_model/ /model/
EXPOSE 8080
CMD ["python", "-m", "vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server", \
     "--model",             "/model", \
     "--port",              "8080", \
     "--served-model-name", "codesage-llama3", \
     "--max-model-len",     "8192", \
     "--dtype",             "bfloat16"]

serving-template.yaml — Step E: SAP AI Core BYOM registration

apiVersion: ai.sap.com/v1alpha1
kind: ServingTemplate
metadata:
  name: codesage-llama3
  labels:
    scenarios.ai.sap.com/id: codesage-scenario
    ai.sap.com/version: "1.0"
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      imagePullSecrets:
        - name: <your-registry-secret>
      containers:
        - name: kserve-container
          image: <your-registry>/codesage-llama3:v1
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "20Gi"
            limits:
              nvidia.com/gpu: "1"    # SAP AI Core allocates a managed GPU

Prerequisites & How to Run

Step A — Generate training data 

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

pip install anthropic
$env:ANTHROPIC_API_KEY = "sk-ant-your-key-here"

python generate_qa.py
# Reads each .abap file, sends to Claude, saves (question, code) pairs

(Get-Content .\training_data.jsonl).Count
# Typical: 30,000 to 50,000 lines

Step B — Fine-tune: choose your GPU option

Option

GPU

Cost

RAM

Duration

Best For

Option A — Kaggle (recommended for first run)

NVIDIA T4 16 GB (free)

£0

16 GB GPU + 13 GB RAM

6–12 hrs

Anyone, no hardware needed

Option B — Local NVIDIA GPU

RTX 3090 (24 GB) / RTX 4090 (24 GB) / A100 (40–80 GB)

Electricity only

24+ GB VRAM recommended

2–6 hrs

Faster iteration, larger batch, keep data on-prem

 Option A — Kaggle free GPU (T4 16 GB)

  1. Upload training_data.jsonl to Kaggle as a Dataset (kaggle.com → Datasets → New Dataset).
  2. Create a new Kaggle Notebook. In the right panel set Accelerator = GPU T4 x1 and enable Internet access.
  3. In the first notebook cell, install dependencies:

# Kaggle Cell 1 — install + HuggingFace login
!pip install -q transformers peft trl bitsandbytes accelerate datasets huggingface_hub
import huggingface_hub
huggingface_hub.login(token="hf_YOUR_READ_TOKEN")

  1. Paste the contents of train_qlora.py into Cell 2 (update the dataset path to your Kaggle input path).
  2. Click Run All. Training takes 6–12 hours. You can close the browser — Kaggle keeps running.
  3. When complete, download the lora-adapter/ folder as ZIP. Extract to C:\codesage\phase3_finetune\lora-adapter\

Option B — Local NVIDIA GPU (RTX 3090 / 4090 / A100)

If you have a Windows machine with a modern NVIDIA GPU you can run the full training pipeline locally. This keeps all training data on-premise (no upload to Kaggle) and is typically 2–3× faster than a T4 due to higher memory bandwidth.

Additional prerequisites for local GPU training:

Step B2.1 — Verify NVIDIA setup in PowerShell

# Check GPU is recognised and driver version
nvidia-smi
# Expected output includes: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 / 4090 / A100
#   Driver Version: 535.xx  |  CUDA Version: 12.x

# Check CUDA toolkit
nvcc --version
# Expected: Cuda compilation tools, release 11.8 (or 12.x)

# If nvcc not found, CUDA Toolkit is not installed.
# Download from: https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda-downloads
# Choose: Windows -> x86_64 -> 11 -> exe (local)

Step B2.2 — Install Python dependencies for local GPU training

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

# Install PyTorch with CUDA support (match your CUDA version)
# For CUDA 11.8:
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118
# For CUDA 12.1:
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121

# Verify GPU is visible to PyTorch
python -c "import torch; print('CUDA available:', torch.cuda.is_available()); print('GPU:', torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))"
# Expected: CUDA available: True  |  GPU: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090

# Install training libraries
pip install transformers peft trl accelerate datasets huggingface_hub

# Install bitsandbytes (4-bit quantisation for QLoRA)
pip install bitsandbytes
# If bitsandbytes gives DLL errors on Windows, use the community Windows build:
pip install bitsandbytes --prefer-binary --extra-index-url=https://jllllll.github.io/bitsandbytes-windows-webui

Step B2.3 — Download LLaMA-3 base model weights

# Log in to HuggingFace (you must have accepted the Meta LLaMA-3 licence first)
# Visit: https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct and click Accept
python -c "import huggingface_hub; huggingface_hub.login(token='hf_YOUR_READ_TOKEN')"

# Download model (~16 GB, one-time)
python -c "
from huggingface_hub import snapshot_download
snapshot_download('meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct',
                  local_dir='C:\\codesage\\phase3_finetune\\base_model')
print('Download complete.')
"

# Check folder size (should be ~16 GB)
(Get-ChildItem -Path ".\base_model" -Recurse | Measure-Object -Property Length -Sum).Sum / 1GB

Step B2.4 — Update train_qlora.py to use the local model path

The only change needed from the Kaggle version is the MODEL_ID path — point it to the local download instead of HuggingFace Hub:

# In train_qlora.py, change this line:
MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"     # Kaggle version (downloads from HF)

# To this for local GPU:
MODEL_ID = "C:\\codesage\\phase3_finetune\\base_model"   # uses local download

# You can also increase batch size on a 24 GB GPU for faster training:
# In TrainingArguments, change:
per_device_train_batch_size=4   # T4 safe value
# To:
per_device_train_batch_size=8   # RTX 3090/4090 with 24 GB VRAM

Step B2.5 — Run training locally

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

# Monitor GPU memory before starting
nvidia-smi

# Run training
python train_qlora.py

# In a second PowerShell window, monitor GPU usage while training runs:
while ($true) { nvidia-smi --query-gpu=utilization.gpu,memory.used,memory.total --format=csv,noheader; Start-Sleep 10 }
# Expected: gpu-util 95-100%,  memory.used ~14000 MiB / 24576 MiB (RTX 3090)

# Training completes in 2-6 hours (vs 6-12 on Kaggle T4)
# Adapter saved to: .\lora-adapter\

Step C — Merge adapter on Windows PC (~20 min, needs 32 GB RAM)

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

pip install transformers peft accelerate
python merge_model.py

# Verify output
Get-ChildItem .\merged_model
# Expected: config.json, tokenizer.json, model-00001-of-00004.safetensors, etc.

Step D — Build and push Docker image (Docker Desktop required)

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune

docker login   # enter Docker Hub credentials
docker build -t codesage-llama3:v1 .    # ~18 GB image, takes 10-20 min first time

docker tag  codesage-llama3:v1  your-dockerhub-username/codesage-llama3:v1
docker push your-dockerhub-username/codesage-llama3:v1  # 30-60 min upload

Step E — Deploy to SAP AI Core (browser, SAP AI Launchpad)

  1. Open SAP AI Launchpad → ML Operations → Docker Registry Secrets → Add (your registry + credentials).
  2. ML Operations → Serving Templates → Upload YAML → select serving-template.yaml.
  3. ML Operations → Configurations → Create → select the codesage-llama3 template.
  4. ML Operations → Deployments → Create → select the configuration. Wait for status: RUNNING (5–10 min).
  5. Copy the Deployment URL and Deployment ID. Store both in BTP → Connectivity → Destinations as destination CODESAGE_AICORE.

Test the endpoint from PowerShell:

# test_aicore.py  -- python test_aicore.py
import requests
token_resp = requests.post("https://<token-url>/oauth/token",
    data={"grant_type": "client_credentials"},
    auth=("<client-id>", "<client-secret>"))
token = token_resp.json()["access_token"]

response = requests.post(
    "https://<ai-core-url>/v2/inference/deployments/<deployment-id>/chat/completions",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "AI-Resource-Group": "default"},
    json={"model": "codesage-llama3",
          "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is ABAP?"}],
          "max_tokens": 200})
print(response.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"])
# Expected: ABAP stands for Advanced Business Application Programming...

Phase 3 Outcome:  A fine-tuned LLaMA-3 8B model — trained on your organisation's ABAP codebase using QLoRA — is running as a live, OpenAI-compatible inference endpoint on SAP AI Core. No local GPU is needed at runtime. Training cost: £0 on Kaggle free tier. The endpoint is reachable from BTP via a secure Destination. Phases 1, 2, and 3 are now all complete — Phase 4 wires them together.

PHASE 4  —  Runtime Query via SAP BTP

Phase 4 is the user-facing layer. It deploys a small Node.js service (BTP CAP) called the CodeSage Agent that orchestrates the full query pipeline:

Step

What Happens

Where

1

Developer types a plain English question in the UI or Teams bot

Browser / Teams

2

Agent embeds the question using nomic-embed-text (local Ollama)

On-premise

3

ChromaDB returns the top-5 most relevant ABAP code chunks

On-premise

4

Agent builds a structured prompt: context chunks + question

BTP CAP service

5

Prompt is sent to the fine-tuned LLaMA-3 on SAP AI Core BYOM

SAP AI Core

6

Answer returned in 2–5 s, citing real program names by name

Browser / Teams

 Artifacts:

Folder Structure

codesage-service.cds — service endpoint definition

// srv/codesage-service.cds
service CodeSageService @(requires: 'CodeSage.User') {

    // POST /codesage/query  { question, context_filter? }
    action query(
        question       : String not null,
        context_filter : String    // optional: 'function_modules' | 'abap_classes' etc.
    ) returns {
        answer      : String;
        sources     : array of { object_name: String; chunk_type: String; };
        model       : String;
        latency_ms  : Integer;
    };
}

query-handler.js — core orchestration: RAG retrieval + AI Core call

// srv/query-handler.js
const axios  = require('axios');
const xsenv  = require('@sap/xsenv');
const { retrieveChunks } = require('./chromadb-client');

const { destination } = xsenv.getServices({ destination: { tag: 'destination' } });

module.exports = (srv) => {
  srv.on('query', async (req) => {
    const { question, context_filter } = req.data;
    const t0 = Date.now();

    // 1. Retrieve top-5 relevant ABAP chunks from on-premise ChromaDB
    const collections = context_filter
      ? [context_filter]
      : ['abap_programs', 'function_modules', 'abap_classes'];
    const chunks  = await retrieveChunks(question, collections, 5);
    const context = chunks
      .map((c, i) => `[${i+1}] ${c.metadata.object} (${c.metadata.type}):\n${c.document}`)
      .join('\n\n');

    // 2. Build prompt
    const messages = [
      { role: 'system',
        content: 'You are CodeSage, an expert ABAP assistant. '
               + 'Answer ONLY using the retrieved context. '
               + 'Cite every object by name. End with a Sources section.' },
      { role: 'user',
        content: `RETRIEVED CONTEXT:\n${context}\n\nQUESTION: ${question}` }
    ];

    // 3. Call SAP AI Core BYOM (OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
    const token    = await getAICoreToken();
    const deployId = process.env.AI_CORE_DEPLOYMENT_ID;
    const response = await axios.post(
      `${destination.url}/v2/inference/deployments/${deployId}/chat/completions`,
      { model: 'codesage-llama3', messages, max_tokens: 1024, temperature: 0.1 },
      { headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`, 'AI-Resource-Group': 'default' } });

    const answer  = response.data.choices[0].message.content;
    const sources = chunks.map(c => ({ object_name: c.metadata.object,
                                       chunk_type:  c.metadata.type }));
    return { answer, sources, model: 'codesage-llama3', latency_ms: Date.now() - t0 };
  });
};

Prerequisites & How to Run

 Step 1 — Install developer tools (one-time)

# Verify Node.js after install
node --version    # Expected: v18.x.x+
npm  --version    # Expected: 9.x.x+

npm install -g @sap/cds-dk
cds --version     # Expected: @sap/cds: 7.x.x

npm install -g mbt
mbt --version     # Expected: 1.2.x

# Install cf CLI from github.com/cloudfoundry/cli/releases (Windows .exe installer)
cf --version      # Expected: cf version 8.x.x

Step 2 — Install Node.js dependencies and test locally

cd C:\codesage\phase4_runtime\codesage-agent
npm install

# Set local env vars for testing
$env:CHROMADB_URL           = "http://localhost:8000"
$env:AI_CORE_DEPLOYMENT_ID  = "<your-deployment-id>"

cds watch
# [cds] - serving CodeSageService at http://localhost:4004/codesage

Test in a second PowerShell window while cds watch is running:

$body = @{ question = "How do we validate vendor payment terms?" } | ConvertTo-Json
Invoke-RestMethod -Method Post -Uri "http://localhost:4004/codesage/query" `
  -Headers @{ "Content-Type" = "application/json" } -Body $body
# Expected: answer + sources + latency_ms

Step 3 — Log in to BTP Cloud Foundry and create service instances

cf login -a https://api.cf.<your-region>.hana.ondemand.com
# Enter BTP email and password, select your org and space

cf create-service xsuaa        application  codesage-xsuaa        -c xs-security.json
cf create-service destination  lite         codesage-destination

cf services
# Both services should show: create succeeded

Step 4 — Build MTA archive and deploy to BTP

mbt build
# Creates: .\mta_archives\codesage-agent_1.0.0.mtar

cf deploy mta_archives\codesage-agent_1.0.0.mtar
# Takes 3-5 minutes. Ends with:
# Application "codesage-agent" started at:
# https://codesage-agent-<id>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Step 5 — Point deployed agent to on-premise ChromaDB

# Use SAP Cloud Connector to expose ChromaDB port to BTP, then:
cf set-env codesage-agent CHROMADB_URL           "https://virtual-chromadb-host:8000"
cf set-env codesage-agent AI_CORE_DEPLOYMENT_ID  "<your-deployment-id>"
cf restage codesage-agent

cf app codesage-agent
# Expected: requested state: started  instances: 1/1

Step 6 — End-to-end live test

# test_live.py  -- python test_live.py
import requests
token_resp = requests.post("<xsuaa-url>/oauth/token",
    data={"grant_type": "client_credentials"},
    auth=("<client-id>", "<client-secret>"))
token = token_resp.json()["access_token"]

response = requests.post(
    "https://codesage-agent-<id>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/codesage/query",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={"question": "Do we have a function module for vendor payment term validation?"})
data = response.json()
print("Answer:",   data["answer"][:200])
print("Sources:",  data["sources"])
print("Latency:", data["latency_ms"], "ms")
# Expected:
# Answer: Yes — Z_VALIDATE_VENDOR_PAYTERMS validates vendor payment terms against T052...
# Sources: [{'object_name': 'Z_VALIDATE_VENDOR_PAYTERMS', 'chunk_type': 'FUNCTION'}]
# Latency: 2841 ms

Step 7 — Register as a Fiori Launchpad tile (optional)

  1. In BTP Cockpit → HTML5 Applications, upload the ui/webapp folder.
  2. In SAP Fiori Launchpad Configuration Cockpit, add a new tile pointing to the HTML5 app URL.
  3. Assign the tile to a role collection mapped to CodeSage.User. Developers see the tile when they open Fiori Launchpad.

Phase 4 Outcome:  A developer types a question in plain English and receives a contextualised ABAP answer in 2–5 seconds, citing real function modules and programs by name. Every query is authenticated via BTP XSUAA, grounded by ChromaDB retrieval, and generated by the fine-tuned LLaMA-3 on SAP AI Core. No source code leaves the SAP BTP perimeter. Developers stop reinventing — they reuse.

Summary — what the developer gets: Every CodeSage answer is a synthesis of three layers: (1) what your organisation has already built — reuse, do not reinvent; (2) what SAP recommends as the correct standard approach; (3) what is safe and compatible for the S/4HANA journey ahead. No other tool in the SAP ecosystem combines all three automatically in a single developer query response.

entry.title: Re: Can a rejected automatic retrofit be reprocessed again?
entry.content:
Hello, 

Once a transport categorized as automatic is rejected manually by a user the transport retrofit status changed to "Manually Retrofitted" and it cannot be retrofitted automatically any more, this manual rejection cannot be reverted.

Check the documentation in the Performing a Retrofit Help Portal content, pay attention to the Rejecting Retrofit Objects section as you could need to remove the created ToC manually.

"Please note that a ToC has been created when the source transport categorization is finished. This means that you have to remove the ToC manually from the import queue if you want to reject a retrofit."

Best regards,

Dolores

entry.title: TPM_Migration necessary for S4HANA conversion if Busness Function FIN_TRM_LR_FI_AN is not active?
entry.content:
My client upgraded to ECC6.0 EHP8 to do a S4HANA conversion (brownfield) afterward. Based on the simplification item check I receive the information that I need to do a migration of a money market produkt into the quantity ledger although I didn't activate the business function FIN_TRM_LR_FI_AN (2270529 - S4TWL - Mengenbuch bei Geldhandelsgeschäften immer aktiv - SAP for Me).

So my question is: do I need to execute the TPM_MIGRATION with conversion type 6 or is this not relevant for the conversion into the SAP S4HANA Private Cloud?

entry.title: Re: Sorting Issue in Billing Document Item Details CDS Hi...
entry.content: In Public Cloud, the standard Invoice List CDS hierarchy does not guarantee sorted Billing Document Items. To display them consistently in your PDF, you need to build a custom CDS view or analytical query that enforces ORDER BY Billing Document, Item Number, and then consume that in your BTP output.

entry.title: Time Dependency in SAP Business Partner: Maintenance and Data Distribution to Non-Time-Dependent App
entry.content:
Time Dependency in Business Partner

Introduction 

In SAP Business Partner, time dependency is a powerful concept that allows master data to be maintained with validity periods. This helps organizations manage historical, current, and future data effectively, with changes applied automatically based on dates. 

While this improves data accuracy and auditability, it also introduces complexity, especially when integrating with systems that do not support time-dependent data 

 
Evolution of Time Dependency 
 
SAP Release 6.40 – Partial Time Dependency 

In SAP Release 6.40, time dependency was partially introduced in SAP Business Partner for specific components, allowing limited use of validity periods in Business Partner data. 

Time dependency was enabled for key areas such as 

  • Bank details (BUT0BK) 
  • Business partner roles (BUT100) 
  • Addresses (BUT020) 
  • Communication data (ADR2 to ADR13, ADRU) 
  • Address usage with full time dependency (BUT021_FS) 

However, since only selected tables supported validity intervals, the concept of time dependency was not uniformly applied across all Business Partner data, resulting in partial time-sliced data management. 

 

SAP Release 7.0 – Full Time Dependency 

In SAP Release 7.0, time dependency was significantly enhanced to provide a more comprehensive and consistent approach to data management in SAP Business Partner. 

Full time dependency was introduced for central data through table BUT000_TD, ensuring that even core Business Partner attributes could be maintained with validity periods. 

Additionally, EEW (Enhancement Framework) extensions were also enabled with time dependency, allowing custom fields to follow the same time-based behavior. 

With these enhancements, SAP achieved complete time-sliced Business Partner data management, where all relevant data can be maintained, tracked, and processed based on validity periods across the entire Business Partner model. 

Steps: 
 
Now let us understand how time dependency is maintained and how data is distributed to non-time-dependent applications. 
 
Time dependency is controlled via customizing table V_TB056, where it can be activated for: 

  • Central Data (BUT000)  
  • Addresses  
  • Bank Details  
  • Roles 

Note: If time dependency is inactive for any dataset (e.g., Address, Bank, Roles), then time slices cannot be maintained for that dataset. 

Time Dependency on Central Data (BUT000)
 
When time dependency is active for central data (Table BUT000), the field validity period automatically appeared during creation or updating with default validity from 01.01.0001 to 31.12.9999. 
 

 
let us create a new validity for the same BP that is from 01.04.2026 to 31.12.9999 and add Search term as SAP, the system automatically splits the existing record as below: 

  • 01.01.0001 → 31.03.2026  
  • 01.04.2026 → 31.12.9999 

Similarly, when future validity is added (e.g., 01.01.2027 → 31.12.2027), the system automatically adjusts intervals without gaps: 

  • 01.01.0001 → 31.03.2026  
  • 01.04.2026 → 31.12.2026  
  • 01.01.2027 → 31.12.2027  
  • 01.01.2028 → 31.12.9999 

 
This is how consistency has maintained for the business Partner Central data when Time dependency is activated for BUT000. 

Now, we will see how this time slices will store in the table when Time dependency is active for the Central data (Table BUT000).  

The currently valid time slice will be stored in the BUT000 table, and the past/future time slice will be stored in the BUT000_TD table. 
 
BUT000 table 

 

BUT000_TD 

Note: Time dependency has been disabled at the header level of BUT000 in SAP S/4HANA and SAP strongly recommends that the end user should NOT maintain the header level data using Customizing to ensure that there are no inconsistencies in the master data. Refer to SAP Note 2379157. 

Now, let us look at other Business Partner datasets such as Addresses, Bank Details, and Roles. 
 
Note: It is important to understand that the concept of time dependency is different for central data (BUT000) compared to other datasets like BUT020, BUT0BK, and BUT100.  

  • For central data (BUT000), the currently valid time slice is stored in the BUT000 table. The past and future time slices for central data are stored separately in the BUT000_TD table. 
  • In contrast, for other datasets such as Addresses, Bank Details, and Roles, all validity records are stored in the same respective table. 

 
Time Dependency for the BP Addresses 

Time dependency for Business Partner addresses allows you to maintain validity periods when it is activated. Once time dependency is active for addresses, each address record can be maintained with a specific valid-from and valid-to date. 
 
When an address is created, the system by default assigns the validity period from the date of creation to end date (31.12.9999), as shown in the screenshot below. 

If a new address is created for the same Business Partner with a different validity period from 07.04.2026 to 31.12.9999 and the previous one changes to 06.04.2026 to 06.04.2026 as shown in the below screen shot. 

As a result, the Business Partner will have multiple address records with different validity periods, and the system determines the Standard address based on the current validity.  
 
Note: The standard address is the one that is automatically picked by applications whenever address data is required for the Business partner. 
 
Note: It is important to note that a Business Partner must always have at least one valid standard address at any given time. 
 
Also, unlike central data (BUT000), all address validity records are stored in the same table as BUT020, where past, current, and future records coexist without any separate time-dependent table. 



Time Dependency for the BP Bank Details 

Time dependency for Business Partner bank details works similarly to addresses. where the validity period is by default maintained from the date of creation (current date) to the maximum end date (31.12.9999). However, this validity period can be adjusted based on business requirements. 
 

Multiple bank details can be maintained for the same Business Partner with different validity periods, as shown in the screenshot below, allowing effective management of time-dependent banking information. 

All such bank detail records, including past, current, and future validities, are stored in the Business Partner bank details table BUT0BK. 

Time Dependency for the BP Roles 

You can create a BP in one or more BP roles. A BP can take part in different business processes simultaneously. For this reason, a BP can have various roles, such as Customer, Supplier, Employee, and so on. 
 
When time dependency is active for roles, each role is maintained with a specific validity period, during which it remains active. Once the validity period expires, the role is automatically deactivated. 

By default, the validity period for a role is maintained from the date of creation (current date) to the maximum end date (31.12.9999), like addresses and bank details, although it can be adjusted based on business requirements. 

You can observe in the below Snap that I have created a vendor role, and its validity is from date of creation [06.04.2026] to end date [31.12.9999]. 

As shown in the screenshot below, a vendor role has been created with validity from 06.04.2026[current date] to 31.12.9999[End-date]. 

In addition, another role such as Disposer has been assigned to the same Business Partner with a different validity period from 06.04.2026 to 31.12.2027. 
 

All such role assignments along with their respective validity periods are stored in the Business Partner roles table BUT100, where multiple role records can coexist for the same Business Partner. 

Distribution of time-dependent business partner data 
 
To understand the distribution of time-dependent Business Partner data, let us consider a Business Partner as shown in the screenshot below, created as a Vendor with two bank details. Bank ID 001 is valid from 31.03.2026 to 14.04.2026, and Bank ID 002 is valid from 15.04.2026 to 31.12.9999. Based on the current date, Bank ID 001 is considered the currently valid record. 

When we check the same vendor in transaction XK03 [non-time dependent application], we can observe that there are no explicit valid-from and valid-to fields displayed. Instead, the system automatically picks and displays the bank detail corresponding to the current validity period, which in this case is Bank ID 001. 

 

How does the system determine the correct current validity record for a Business Partner in non-time-dependent applications? 
 
The system determines the correct current validity record using the report BUPTDTRANSMIT. 
 
How does this report work? 

The report BUPTDTRANSMIT is triggered via the technical job SAP_BUPA_BUPTDTRANSMIT every day at midnight to distribute any BP data that becomes valid on that current day to the related non-time-dependent system or application. 
 

  • When Business Partner data is created or updated, any records with future validity dates are not immediately distributed to other systems.  
  • These future-valid records are stored in a separate table called BUT_TDTRANS to prevent sending data prematurely to systems that do not support time dependency. 
  • When the BUPTDTRANSMIT report runs, it reads the records from BUT_TDTRANS and selects only those records that are valid for the current date. 
  • The report then distributes these valid records to external systems, internal applications, or any relevant Business Partner processes. 
  • In some scenarios, the report may fail to execute or may not successfully distribute records on a particular day. 
  • If the distribution fails, those records are stored in the BUT_TDMISS table, and the target systems or applications do not receive the data. 
  • The records in BUT_TDMISS remain there until they are successfully processed in a subsequent execution of the report. 
  • Once all records are successfully processed and distributed, they are deleted from BUT_TDTRANS.  
  • Any corresponding entries in BUT_TDMISS are also removed after successful processing, ensuring data consistency across systems. 

Conclusion 

Time dependency in SAP Business Partner enables efficient management of past, current, and future data while ensuring consistency through proper validity handling. 

It also supports seamless integration with non-time-dependent systems using BUT_TDTRANS, BUT_TDMISS, and the BUPTDTRANSMIT report, ensuring only valid data is distributed. 

Overall, it improves data accuracy and ensures reliable synchronization across systems.
